ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term for chicken pho; closest parent is chicken soup (FOODON_00002700).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (chicken soup with noodles) used as closest parent; no dedicated pho code exists in FoodEx2.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of chicken pho weighing approximately 500 g, comprising broth, rice noodles, shredded chicken, bean sprouts, and herbs.

nutrient_source.title: Pho ga (Vietnamese chicken noodle soup) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite; cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Vietnamese noodle soups
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for chicken pho as a composite dish. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for chicken broth, cooked rice noodles, shredded poached chicken breast, bean sprouts, and herbs, weighted to a typical restaurant-style bowl composition (approximately 35% broth, 30% rice noodles, 20% chicken, 10% bean sprouts, 5% herbs/garnish). Cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA data for Vietnamese pho-style soups. Per-100g values reflect the mixed edible portion.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice noodles and mixed noodle soup analogues
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken pho as a composite dish. GI of 40 is estimated using the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice noodles in soup context (GI approximately 40, low category), consistent with published values for rice-noodle-based soups. The protein and fat content of the broth and chicken further attenuate the glycemic response. Glycemic load calculated as (GI x available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (40 x 39.0 g) / 100 = 15.6. Available carbohydrate per 500 g serving = 7.8 x 5 = 39.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from broth and fish sauce
hypertension.guidance: A 500 g bowl of chicken pho provides approximately 1900 mg sodium, which represents 83% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium broth, limit the broth consumed, and avoid adding extra fish sauce or soy sauce at the table.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice noodles, partially offset by protein and broth
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 40 and a glycemic load of 15.6 per bowl, chicken pho is a moderate-GL meal for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; portion control of the rice noodle component and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables can help attenuate postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content of chicken pho broth poses a cardiovascular risk for individuals with existing heart disease; choosing lean shredded chicken breast, consuming only half the broth, and requesting low-sodium preparation can reduce sodium intake to a more cardioprotective level.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken pho (Vietnamese: Phở gà) is a traditional Vietnamese noodle soup dish consisting of a clear, aromatic chicken broth simmered with spices including star anise, cinnamon, ginger, and cloves, served over flat rice noodles (bánh phở) and topped with tender shredded poached chicken breast, fresh bean sprouts, spring onions, fresh herbs such as Thai basil and coriander, and a squeeze of lime. The broth is the defining element, requiring long simmering to develop its characteristic golden colour and complex savoury-sweet flavour. In Singapore, chicken pho is commonly served at Vietnamese restaurants and food delivery platforms as a lighter alternative to beef pho. Nutritionally, a standard 500 g bowl provides approximately 310 kcal, 29 g protein, 6 g fat, and 39 g carbohydrate, making it a relatively high-protein, moderate-carbohydrate meal. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. The primary nutritional concern is sodium, with a typical bowl containing approximately 1900 mg from the broth and fish sauce seasoning, which is clinically significant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 40 (low), making it a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when portion size is managed. The dish is rich in B vitamins from the chicken and provides iron, zinc, and phosphorus. Bean sprouts contribute vitamin C and dietary fibre. Chicken pho is considered a comforting, restorative meal across Vietnamese culinary tradition and is widely consumed as a breakfast, lunch, or dinner dish.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a bowl of chicken pho?
Is chicken pho high in sodium?
What is the difference between chicken pho and beef pho?
How much protein is in chicken pho?
Can I eat pho if I have high blood pressure?
What are the ingredients in chicken pho?

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains a direct entry for chicken pho as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for constituent ingredients weighted to a typical bowl composition, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA data for Vietnamese noodle soups. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using rice noodles in soup context as the closest analogous food. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the component sources used for this estimated composite dish. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA component data used for this estimate. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible in this dish as it contains no hydrogenated fats. Sodium value of 380 mg per 100g reflects a typical restaurant-style broth with fish sauce seasoning; home-prepared versions may have substantially lower sodium. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate based on rice noodles in soup context from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for chicken pho as a composite dish was found in approved sources. The food is classified under Singapore cuisine in the input record but is of Vietnamese culinary origin; both are reflected in the record. Multilingual names include Vietnamese (Phở gà) as the language of origin, plus the four mandatory Singaporean languages (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) per G6 rules for a Singapore-served dish.
